title: "Cheese!": a corpus of face-to-face French interactions. A case study for analyzing smiling and conversational humor
venue: Proceedings of the 12th Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC 2020), pages 467–475
research_questions: How was the Cheese! multimodal French face-to-face conversational corpus collected and enriched?
Does smiling have an impact on its success or failure?
tasks: analysis of smiling as a marker of humor

datasets_used.name: CHEESE!
datasets_used.size: 11 French face-to-face conversations lasting around 15 minutes each; 22 participants

datasets_used.name: CHEESE!
datasets_used.size: 5 interactions enriched with annotations; 2 interactions (MA_PC and JS_CL) manually annotated for smiles and humor

methods_proposed.name: CHEESE! corpus collection protocol
methods_proposed.summary: The paper describes a multimodal French dyadic conversation collection protocol: participants sit face-to-face in a soundproof room, read canned jokes to each other, and then converse freely until the end of the interaction. The corpus uses separated high-quality audio recordings and front-facing video for each participant.

methods_proposed.name: CHEESE! annotation pipeline
methods_proposed.summary: The corpus is enriched through automatic IPU segmentation with SPPAS, manual enriched orthographic transcription and verification, automatic token/phoneme/syllable/activity/POS-related annotations with SPPAS and MarsaTag, and manual annotations of smiles and humor.

methods_proposed.name: Smiling and humor exploratory analysis
methods_proposed.summary: For two interactions, the paper compares humorous vs. non-humorous sequences and failed vs. successful humorous items using smile presence, facial behavior categories, and mean smile intensity.

humor_theories.detail: Humor recognition criteria include General Theory of Verbal Humor notions such as “script opposition” and “logical mechanism”.

Failed humor is classified through negative reactions: humor acknowledged but answered seriously (A), humor ignored (I), and humor explicitly rejected (R); items are successful when none of these reactions are observed.

evaluation_methods.detail: Manual orthographic transcription was performed by one author and verified by another; smiles in two interactions were manually annotated with the Smiling Intensity Scale; humorous sequences/items were manually annotated by one author.
Cohen’s Kappa assessed smile double-coding reliability: 0.87 for MA_PC and 0.89 for JS_CL.
The exploratory study uses descriptive counts, percentages, durations, and mean smiling intensities to compare humorous/non-humorous and failed/successful humor.

human_samples.population: students in Linguistics at Aix-Marseille University; the two participants of each interaction were in the same class and were friends outside the university; all were French native students, from 20 to 40 years old
human_samples.observations: 11 mixed and non-mixed dyadic interactions lasting around 15 minutes each
human_samples.detail: Participants read each other a canned joke chosen by the researchers and then spoke freely until the end of the interaction; they signed written consent before recordings and were informed of the purpose after data collection was entirely finished.

human_samples.observations: 5 interactions / 10 audio files were manually transcribed and verified
human_samples.detail: One author performed the orthographic transcription; another author verified the IPUs and the orthographic transcription.

human_samples.observations: 2,610 smile intensities were annotated in MA_PC and 2,475 in JS_CL
human_samples.detail: Smiles were annotated in ELAN on 400 ms intervals using the Smiling Intensity Scale; double coding on both interactions yielded Cohen’s Kappa 0.87 for MA_PC and 0.89 for JS_CL.

human_samples.observations: Humorous sequences and humorous items were annotated in MA_PC and JS_CL; item counts by participant were MA 23, PC 32, JS 39, and CL 53
human_samples.detail: The annotator classified humorous sequences as canned joke or conversational humor, annotated humorous items within conversational humor, and classified items as failed or successful based on reactions.

judges.detail: One author manually performed orthographic transcription; another author verified the IPUs and transcription.
Two judges double-coded smile intensities on 400 ms intervals for MA_PC and JS_CL; agreement was reported with Cohen’s Kappa.
One author manually annotated humorous sequences/items and classified conversational humor items as failed or successful based on reactions.

judges.name: SPPAS and MarsaTag
judges.detail: SPPAS generated IPUs and time-aligned token, phoneme, syllable and activity tiers; MarsaTag generated POS-related tiers.

generation_selection.detail: The paper introduces and analyzes a human conversation corpus; it does not generate humor with a model.

inference_budgets.detail: No computational model inference is run; SPPAS and MarsaTag are used for corpus annotation rather than humor generation or evaluation.

human_baselines.detail: The paper does not evaluate computational humor systems, so no human baseline is applicable.

contamination_risk.basis: No LLM or other humor model is evaluated; the paper introduces and analyzes a human interaction corpus.

llm_judge_dependence.detail: No LLMs are used as judges or evaluators.

reporting_gaps: Only 5 of 11 interactions had been enriched with annotations at the time of the paper, and only 2 interactions had manual smile and humor annotations.
Humor annotation was performed by one author; no inter-annotator agreement is reported for humorous sequence/item labels or failed/successful humor labels.
The paper reports double coding for smile annotations but does not specify coder identities, expertise, or blinding.
The exploratory analysis uses descriptive statistics only and reports no statistical significance tests.
The authors identify possible gender and conversational-topic effects, but these are not controlled in the two-interaction exploratory study.

headline_results.finding: CHEESE! contains 11 French face-to-face conversations with 22 participants, and 5 interactions had been enriched with annotations at the time of publication.
headline_results.value: 11 French face-to-face conversations lasting around 15 minutes each; 22 participants; 5 interactions enriched with annotations

headline_results.finding: Smile annotation reliability was qualified as excellent for both manually smile-coded interactions.
The 5 annotated dialogues contain a restricted vocabulary, with many singleton words and a small set of frequent words covering much of the speech.
headline_results.value: 2,130 different words representing 20,201 occurrences; 1,027 (48.2%) occur only once; the 10 most frequent words occur 4,817 times and cover 23.8%; 40 words covers about 50% of all spoken words

headline_results.finding: The two analyzed interactions differed strongly in the amount of humorous production and participant humor frequency.
headline_results.metric: humorous production duration and humorous items per participant
headline_results.value: 2.6 min in MA_PC for a total interaction lasting 17.4 min; 7.03 min in JS_CL for a total interaction lasting 16.5 min; MA 23 (1.3/min), PC 32 (1.8/min), JS 39 (2.36/min), CL 53 (3.2/min)

headline_results.finding: Mean smiling intensity was higher during humorous than non-humorous segments for three of four participants, while CL showed no difference.
headline_results.metric: mean smiling intensity during humorous vs non-humorous segments

headline_results.finding: Failed humor was less frequent than successful humor, but smile presence alone did not show that smiling reduces the risk of failure; mean smiling intensity was higher for successful humor for three of four participants.
headline_results.metric: failed/successful item counts and mean smiling intensity Fa/Su
headline_results.value: Failed/Successful items: MA 4/19, PC 13/19, JS 7/32, CL 5/48; mean smiling intensity Fa/Su: MA 2,5/2,03, PC 1,81/2,45, JS 1,8/2,3, CL 0,8/2,2

key_findings: CHEESE! is a multimodal corpus of 11 French face-to-face dyadic interactions with high-quality separated audio and front-facing video, designed for studying smiling and humor in conversation.
The paper’s exploratory study analyzes only conversational humor, excluding the initial canned-joke sequences from the humor-duration analysis.
Smiling is the most frequent facial behavior during humor production for all four analyzed participants, occurring more often than neutral face or laughter.
Mean smiling intensity is generally higher in humorous than non-humorous sequences, but this pattern is not systematic because CL has the same mean intensity in both conditions.
Smile presence is common in both successful and failed humor, so the study does not show that smiling by itself reduces humor failure.
Mean smiling intensity may be more informative than smile presence for success/failure, but the evidence is too limited because the result holds for only three of four participants.
limitations: The exploratory smiling-and-humor analysis is based on only two interactions and four participants.
Only 5 of 11 corpus interactions had enriched annotations, and only 2 had manual smile and humor annotations at the time of publication.
Humor annotation was performed by one author and no inter-annotator reliability for humor labels is reported.
The authors caution that one exception among only four participants creates too much uncertainty to draw clear conclusions about smiling intensity and humor success/failure.
Potential gender effects and conversational-topic effects are suggested but not tested or controlled.
The analysis is descriptive and does not report statistical significance testing.
safety_ethics_notes: All participants signed a written consent form before the recordings and were informed of the purpose of the data collection after it was entirely finished. The paper states that a 46-second demo is publicly available, the whole corpus can be shared on demand after registration, and downsampled audio plus reduced-size video files are freely downloadable without registration.

artifacts.other: A 46-second demo is publicly available; the whole corpus can be shared on-demand after registration; 8,000 Hz audio files and 363x206 px video files are publicly available without registration.
